title: Exposure Reduction, Regulatory Survey Requirements, and Time Limits
narrationScript: You can read a meter — this lesson is about what the readings buy you. First, exposure reduction: time, distance, and shielding applied as field habits. Set up while the source is shielded, crank briskly, work at the full length of the drive cable, put a collimator on the exposure head, keep structure between you and the source, and let the meter choose your position. Second, the regulatory backbone. After every exposure, 10 CFR 34.49 requires a survey of the exposure device and the entire guide tube before anyone touches film or equipment — it is the rule that catches stuck and disconnected sources. Part 20 defines radiation areas, high radiation areas, and very high radiation areas, dictates the exact posting words for each, and requires control of every entrance — in field radiography, continuous direct visual surveillance. Finally, time limits: stay time equals the dose budget divided by the measured dose rate. Measure at the work position, do the division, assign a timekeeper, and leave when the time is up.

sections.heading: Working All Three Levers in the Field
sections.body:
Module 1 taught time, distance, and shielding as physics. On the job they are habits — and the survey meter is what turns the physics into an actual position, pace, and plan for every shot.

- **Time.** Do everything that can be done while the source sits in its shield: film and IQIs placed, collimator set, guide tube routed and anchored, crank path walked clear. From the moment the source leaves the device until it seats in the exposure head — and again on the way home — it is out of its shield and, in transit, outside the collimator. Crank **briskly and smoothly** in both directions. While the exposure runs there is nothing to adjust, so interpretation, paperwork, and conversation happen away from the dose, not inside it.
- **Distance.** Work at the **full length of the drive cable** — never coiled short for convenience. The inverse square law pays you for every step: put the crank position behind or to the side of the device, never in line with the planned beam direction, and remember the cheapest dose reduction on any site is usually one more step back.
- **Shielding.** A collimator on the exposure head cuts dose rates outside the useful beam by an order of magnitude or more — use one whenever the geometry allows. Point the beam into the work, the ground, or heavy structure and away from occupied areas, and keep existing plant steel and concrete between you and the source.

The meter referees all of it: your waiting position during an exposure is wherever the reading is lowest **while you can still see the device and the entire boundary**.

sections.caption: Plan view of a field shot with all three levers working: setup done while the source is shielded, the crank at full cable length behind structure, a collimated beam aimed away from the crew, and a posted boundary watched the entire time.

sections.heading: The Post-Exposure Survey: 10 CFR 34.49
sections.body:
One survey stands above every other in this trade. **10 CFR 34.49** requires surveys to be made with a **calibrated, operable survey instrument** (the §34.25 meter you learned to check in the last lesson), and requires that **after each exposure** the radiographer survey **the exposure device and the guide tube while approaching them**. The survey must determine that the sealed source has returned to its shielded position **before** anyone exchanges film, repositions the exposure head, or dismantles equipment.

Done properly, that means:

- Meter **on, battery-checked, and held ahead of you** before you start in — and watch the trend the whole way: readings should fall toward the device's normal shielded profile, not climb.
- Walk the **complete circumference** of the exposure device.
- Sweep the **entire length of the guide tube**, all the way out to the exposure head — many Agreement-State regulations spell out exactly that sweep.
- Know your device's **normal shielded-profile readings**, and treat anything above them as a stuck or disconnected source until proven otherwise.

The same rule requires a survey of the exposure device **whenever it is placed in storage** and **at every source exchange**, to confirm the source is in its shielded position.

Why the ceremony? Because a drive cable can disconnect and a source can hang up in the tube while everything else looks and feels normal — the crank can turn freely and the device looks exactly as it should. The industry's overexposure case files repeat one root cause more than any other: **the post-exposure survey was skipped or shortcut.**

sections.caption: The post-exposure survey required by 10 CFR 34.49: approach with the meter leading, walk the complete circumference of the exposure device, then sweep the entire guide tube out to the exposure head — before film, hands, or breakdown.

sections.title: No survey, no approach — no exceptions
sections.body: An exposure device with the source stuck at the tip of the guide tube looks **identical** to one safely retracted — and the crank may have turned normally the whole way in. The calibrated, battery-checked meter held in front of you is the only witness that counts. If readings climb above the device's normal shielded profile: **stop, back out along your approach path, secure the boundary, and go straight to your emergency procedures.** No film exchange, no equipment breakdown, no second opinions from the crank.

sections.heading: What the Regulations Require: Surveys, Posting, Control, Records
sections.body:
The framework behind these habits sits in **10 CFR Part 20** (radiation protection standards) and **10 CFR Part 34** (industrial radiography), adopted in compatible form by Agreement States — your license and operating procedures fill in the site-specific details.

- **Surveys — 10 CFR 20.1501.** Every licensee must make the surveys necessary to comply with Part 20 and reasonable to evaluate radiation levels and potential hazards, using calibrated instruments. For a radiography crew that means boundary surveys when the restricted area is established, a survey during the first exposure to verify the boundary — the dose in any unrestricted area beyond it may not exceed **2 mrem in any one hour** (§20.1301) — and the §34.49 device surveys after every shot.
- **Posting — 10 CFR 20.1902 and 34.53.** Signs bear the radiation symbol and exact words: **CAUTION, RADIATION AREA**; **CAUTION (or DANGER), HIGH RADIATION AREA**; **GRAVE DANGER, VERY HIGH RADIATION AREA**. During radiography, posting is never optional: §34.53 requires the area posted per §20.1902(a) and (b), and the posting exceptions of §20.1903 **do not apply** to radiographic operations.
- **Control of high radiation areas — 10 CFR 20.1601.** Each entrance or access point needs one of: a control device that drops the radiation level on entry, a device that energizes a conspicuous visible or audible alarm on entry, or locked entryways with positive control over each entry — or, in place of those, **continuous direct or electronic surveillance capable of preventing unauthorized entry**. Field radiography runs on that last idea: under **§34.51**, the radiographer or the second crew member required by §34.41(a) maintains **continuous direct visual surveillance** of the operation, except at permanent radiographic installations meeting §34.33.
- **Records — 10 CFR 20.2103.** Survey results are records: date, location, instrument make, model, serial number and calibration date, the readings and where they were taken, and who took them — retained for **three years**.

sections.caption: Area classifications under 10 CFR Part 20 — the thresholds, the exact posting words, and the required control.
rows: Dose from external sources may not exceed 2 mrem (0.02 mSv) in any one hour (10 CFR 20.1301)
Open access — your boundary survey proves the limit is met
Could receive >5 mrem (0.05 mSv) in 1 hour at 30 cm from the source or penetrated surface
Inside the restricted-area boundary, under licensee control
Could receive >100 mrem (1 mSv) in 1 hour at 30 cm
"CAUTION, HIGH RADIATION AREA" or "DANGER, HIGH RADIATION AREA"
10 CFR 20.1601 entry-control device, entry alarm, or locked entryways — or continuous direct visual surveillance per 10 CFR 34.51
Absorbed dose could exceed 500 rad (5 Gy) in 1 hour at 1 m
10 CFR 20.1602 — measures that prevent unauthorized or inadvertent entry

sections.heading: Establishing Time Limits: Stay Time
sections.body:
Every dose limit and every ALARA budget converts into minutes through one piece of arithmetic:

**Stay time = allowed dose ÷ measured dose rate**

The *allowed dose* comes from your program, working down from the regulatory ceiling: 5 rem per year occupational TEDE is the legal limit, but licensees assign far lower **administrative levels** — weekly allotments on the order of 100 mrem, or a per-job budget set by the RSO. The *dose rate* comes from your meter at the **actual working position** — where your trunk will really be — never from an assumption or yesterday's survey. For gamma field work, the meter's mR/h and the budget's mrem are treated as equivalent.

Worked straight from the field:

- A 50 mR budget for a boundary fix where the position reads 20 mR/h → 50 ÷ 20 = **2.5 hours** maximum.
- A source-recovery support position reads 250 mR/h against a 25 mR budget → 25 ÷ 250 = 0.1 h = **6 minutes**.
- The job needs 30 minutes and the budget is 25 mR → the position may read no more than **50 mR/h**: add distance or shielding until the meter agrees, or change the plan.

The discipline around the number matters as much as the number: assign a timekeeper, leave when the time is up regardless of how the job stands, and **re-survey and recompute** the moment the source, the shielding, or your position changes. A stay time is a ceiling — ALARA means coming in under it, not riding it.

sections.caption: Stay time is the dose budget divided by the measured dose rate: on a 100 mR budget, 5 mR/h buys 20 hours of working time, while 200 mR/h buys 30 minutes.

figures.title: Exposure reduction at a field radiography shot (plan view)
The post-exposure survey path per 10 CFR 34.49
Stay time versus dose rate on a fixed dose budget

interactive.title: Last Shot of the Night
interactive.intro: Night shift at a pipeline fabrication yard. You and your assistant are shooting girth welds with an Ir-192 exposure device, working a posted boundary you established and verified by survey. Make the calls that keep the crew's doses where they belong.

steps.prompt: The exposure timer ends and you crank the source back. The crank turned smoothly and the cable counter shows full retraction. What happens before anyone moves toward the device?
options: Send your assistant ahead to pull the film while you log the exposure
Pick up the survey meter, confirm it responds, and approach with it held ahead of you, watching the reading the whole way in
Wait one minute to let residual radiation in the area die down, then walk in
Check the device's source-position indicator — if it shows shielded, the survey is optional
steps.feedback: Smooth cranking, a happy counter, and a shielded indicator can all coexist with a disconnected or stuck source. 10 CFR 34.49 requires a survey of the device and guide tube after each exposure, with a calibrated, operable instrument — and there is no 'residual radiation' to wait out: if the source is shielded the field is already down, and if it is not, waiting changes nothing.

steps.prompt: You are at the device, meter in hand, readings falling as expected. What must the survey cover before anyone touches film, head, or guide tube?
options: One reading taken at arm's length from where you stopped
The device surface on the side facing the crank position
The complete circumference of the exposure device and the entire length of the guide tube out to the exposure head
Only the exposure head, because that is where the source was last
steps.feedback: A source can hang up anywhere along the path — inside the device, mid-tube, or at the head. The survey proves the whole path is cold: full circumference of the device, full length of the guide tube, with readings at the device's normal shielded profile before film exchange, repositioning, or breakdown.

steps.prompt: The next exposure runs 25 minutes. Your lowest-reading waiting position that still gives full view of the boundary reads 12 mR/h, and the personal budget your RSO set for the rest of the night is 30 mR. What does the arithmetic say?
options: About 5 mR for this shot — well inside budget; take it from there and keep watching the boundary
The position is illegal — no one may occupy any spot reading over 2 mR/h
No calculation is needed, because stay-time math only applies inside high radiation areas
30 ÷ 12 means all remaining work must finish within 12 minutes
steps.feedback: Dose = rate × time: 12 mR/h × 25/60 h = 5 mR. The 2 mrem-in-any-one-hour figure is the limit for unrestricted areas beyond the boundary — inside the restricted area, monitored workers manage occupancy by stay time. And 30 ÷ 12 = 2.5 hours of total occupancy at that rate, not 12 minutes — but only if you keep re-checking the meter.

steps.prompt: Mid-shift, the client parks a scissor lift that blocks your crew's view of one stretch of the boundary during exposures. What is the call?
options: Keep shooting — the posted signs still control the area
Have your assistant listen for anyone approaching from that side
Stop exposures until the crew can again maintain continuous direct visual surveillance of the operation — reposition, or have the lift moved
Shorten each exposure so any intruder's dose would stay small
steps.feedback: At a temporary jobsite the high-radiation-area control requirement is met by continuous direct visual surveillance under 10 CFR 34.51 — signs inform, but surveillance is what actually prevents unauthorized entry. Lose the sight line and you have lost the control: no exposures until you can see the whole operation again.

checkQuestions.question: Per 10 CFR 34.49, when must the radiographic exposure device and its guide tube be surveyed?
options: Only at the start and end of each shift
Once per day, when the device is first removed from storage
After each exposure, before exchanging film, repositioning the exposure head, or dismantling equipment
Only when the crank feels unusual during retraction
checkQuestions.explanation: The post-exposure survey is required after every single exposure, while approaching the device and guide tube, and must prove the source is back in its shielded position before anyone touches film or equipment — a normal-feeling crank proves nothing.

checkQuestions.question: During a boundary check you find a location where a person could receive 150 mrem in 1 hour at 30 cm from the source. That location must be treated as a:
options: Radiation area
High radiation area
Very high radiation area
Unrestricted area, as long as no one is standing there
checkQuestions.explanation: A high radiation area is any accessible area where an individual could receive more than 100 mrem (1 mSv) in 1 hour at 30 cm. The very-high classification requires a potential absorbed dose over 500 rad in 1 hour at 1 m — an entirely different magnitude.

checkQuestions.question: Your RSO assigns a 50 mR dose budget for a task at a position where the survey meter reads 20 mR/h. The maximum stay time is:
options: 2.5 hours
25 minutes
2 hours
40 minutes
checkQuestions.explanation: Stay time = allowed dose ÷ measured dose rate = 50 mR ÷ 20 mR/h = 2.5 hours — valid only at that measured position, and only until the source, shielding, or geometry changes, which forces a re-survey and a new calculation.

checkQuestions.question: At a temporary jobsite, how does an industrial radiography crew satisfy the requirement to control access to the high radiation area?
options: Posting the boundary with the required signs satisfies the control requirement by itself
Every field site must install a control device that automatically retracts the source on entry
The crew may leave the area unattended once signs and rope are in place
By maintaining continuous direct visual surveillance of the operation, per 10 CFR 34.51
checkQuestions.explanation: 10 CFR 20.1601 allows continuous direct surveillance in place of control devices, alarms, or locked entries, and 10 CFR 34.51 makes it the working rule for field radiography: the radiographer or the second required crew member watches the operation continuously. Signs inform people — surveillance is the control.

checkQuestions.question: Which sign wording does 10 CFR 20.1902 require at a very high radiation area?
options: "DANGER, HIGH RADIATION AREA"
"GRAVE DANGER, VERY HIGH RADIATION AREA"
"CAUTION, EXTREME RADIATION AREA"
"RESTRICTED AREA — AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY"
checkQuestions.explanation: Each area class has exact required wording: CAUTION, RADIATION AREA; CAUTION (or DANGER), HIGH RADIATION AREA; and GRAVE DANGER, VERY HIGH RADIATION AREA for areas where the absorbed dose could exceed 500 rad in 1 hour at 1 meter.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Appendix A §3.4–3.6 (exposure reduction, regulatory survey requirements, establishment of time limits)
10 CFR Part 20, Standards for Protection Against Radiation (§§20.1003, 20.1301, 20.1501, 20.1601, 20.1602, 20.1902, 20.2103)
10 CFR Part 34, Licenses for Industrial Radiography and Radiation Safety Requirements for Industrial Radiographic Operations (§§34.41, 34.49, 34.51, 34.53)
